lt.gd: This bill creates certain requirements and accountability procedures regarding a student's use of software in a public school.
lt.hp: This bill:defines terms;requires the State Board of Education to:create a statewide digital privacy agreement for educational software;ensure that all software used in a public school is executed under the statewide digital privacy agreement and academically effective;create a master list for software used in public schools;create a process for a vendor to certify that the vendor's software is academically effective;create a list of approved software;verify software as academically effective;enforce compliance with the requirements of this section through periodic audits;create a process for a parent to submit a complaint; andcreate rules to implement the requirements of this section;creates exceptions for the statewide digital privacy agreement;requires a local education agency (LEA), the Utah Education and Telehealth Network, and the State Board of Education to:execute the statewide digital privacy agreement for all software contracts; andverify certain software is academically effective;requires the use of a digital privacy agreement for administrative software;allows a vendor to offer educational software to a contracting entity on a provisional basis;requires an LEA to notify parents of all software a public school uses during the school year;requires the state board to consult with the Office of the Attorney General; andrequires a vendor to execute the statewide digital privacy agreement for all software contracts with a contracting entity.
lt.oc: Other Special Clauses:This bill provides a special effective date.
lt.sa: Utah Code Sections Affected:ENACTS:53G-7-1401, Utah Code Annotated 195353G-7-1402, Utah Code Annotated 195353G-7-1403, Utah Code Annotated 195353G-7-1404, Utah Code Annotated 195353G-7-1405, Utah Code Annotated 195353G-7-1406, Utah Code Annotated 195353G-7-1407, Utah Code Annotated 1953

leg.enact: Be it enacted by the Legislature of the state of Utah:

section.secline: Section 1. Section 53G-7-1401 is enacted to read:

subsection.amend: "Academic progress" means advancement toward mastery of state academic standards through practice, application, feedback, or demonstration of knowledge or skill.
"Academically effective" means software that:
is designed to provide support, or enable student active learning, skill development, or academic progress in the intended subject area;
where applicable, aligns with the public education core standards described in Section 53E-4-202;
does not employ design features that primarily:
interfere with active learning; or
undermine teacher instructional authority; and
contributes to or enables measurable academic progress or skill development when used as intended.
"Active learning" means instruction that requires a student to engage in cognitive processes including analyzing, reasoning, practicing, or creating to understand or apply knowledge or skills.
"Addictive design feature" means a feature or component of a digital or online product that encourages or increases a student's frequency, time spent, or engagement with the product.
"Addictive design feature" includes the following features:
infinite scroll;
autoplay that continues beyond the educational task or lesson;
points, badges, or other gamification rewards tied to time spent on the product rather than learning objectives or academic progress;
persistent notifications prompting re-engagement when the product is not actively in use, unless:
a teacher initiates the notification; and
the notification is directly related to assigned schoolwork;
personalized recommendation systems designed to maximize time-on-platform rather than learning outcomes; or
engagement metrics, streaks, or social comparison features designed to create fear of missing out or compulsive checking behavior.
"Addictive design feature" does not include a:
recommendation of next lessons or learning activities based on curriculum progression or mastery of prerequisites;
notification about a teacher-assigned or course-required assignment, deadline, or teacher feedback; or
feature that encourages active learning rather than passive consumption.
"Administrative software" means software that a contracting entity uses solely for administrative, operational, or other non-instructional functions of the contracting entity.
"Clickstream data" means data an LEA or third-party provider collects from a student's use of an online service, application, or device that records the student's navigation or sequence of actions.
"Contracting entity" means the following entities if that entity contracts with a vendor for software:
an LEA;
the state board; or
UETN.
"Digital privacy agreement" means a contract between a contracting entity and a digital provider that:
ensures compliance with Title 53E, Chapter 9, Student Privacy and Data Protection; and
governs access, use, protection, retention, and disclosure of student data.
"Educational purpose" means a purpose directly related to:
student instruction;
assessment of a student; or
school operations necessary for instruction of a student.
"Educational purpose" does not include:
marketing;
advertising;
behavioral profiling; or
any other commercial purpose.
"Educational software" means software that:
serves an educational purpose;
is designed for student instruction, assessment, or instructional support;
is executed under the statewide digital privacy agreement; and
is verified for academic effectiveness in accordance with the requirements of this section.

"Independently verified" means the characteristic of software that an impartial third party with demonstrated expertise appropriate to the software type, whom the vendor does not own, control or direct, and whose compensation does not depend on the evaluation outcome, evaluates for:
academic effectiveness; and
compliance with the requirements of this part.
"Internet service provider" means the same as that term is defined in Section 76-5c-401.

"Passive consumption" means receiving information through viewing, listening, or browsing without requiring the student to engage in cognitive processing necessary to analyze, apply, or create knowledge or skills.
"School-issued device" means any electronic hardware device an LEA provides to a student for educational use.

"Skill development" means the acquisition or improvement of academic abilities or competencies necessary to perform a task aligned to state academic standards, including guided practice, modeling, or feedback.
"Software" means any application, web-based service, plug-in, or other code-based product, regardless of whether the application is free or for purchase, that:
runs on or is accessible from a school-issued device; and

an LEA assigns, requires, recommends, installs, or otherwise makes available for student use in connection with classroom instruction, including through a school-issued account or identity, regardless or whether the software is accessible on a school-issued or student-owned device.
"Software" includes software an individual uses during school hours in connection with school-related purposes for:
instruction;
assessment;
communication;
collaboration; or
enrichment.
"Software" does not include physical, electronic hardware.
"Statewide digital privacy agreement" means the digital privacy agreement the state board creates in accordance with Section 53G-7-1402.
"Student data" means the same as that term is defined in Section 53E-9-301.
"Student data" includes a student's:
personal data as that term is defined in Section 13-61-101;
metadata, device identifiers, and clickstream data;
behavioral, engagement, or usage data; and
information a software collects, generates, or infers in the course of student use.
"Sub-processor" means a third-party vendor or service that a primary data processor engages to process personal data on the processor's behalf.

"Telecommunications carrier" means an entity that provides transmission, routing, or connectivity services for digital communications, including wireless, broadband, or data transport services, without modifying the content of communications.
"Telecommunications carrier" includes an internet service provider.
"Utah Education and Telehealth Network" or "UETN" means the same as that term is defined in Section 53H-4-213.1.
"Vendor" means an entity that provides software, digital tools, digital services, or related technology to a contracting entity for student use, whether free or paid.
"Vendor" does not include:
a telecommunications carrier; or
an internet service provider.
"Voice-print" means a digital representation of an individual's voice that a person creates, derives, or uses to identify or authenticate the individual.

section.secline: Section 2. Section 53G-7-1402 is enacted to read:

subsection.amend: The state board shall create a form statewide digital privacy agreement, before July 1, 2027, that:
governs student use of educational software and digital services in a public school for:
kindergarten through grade 6; and
core standards, described in Section 53E-4-202, in grades 7 through 12;
complies with the requirements of Title 53E, Chapter 9, Student Privacy and Data Protection, including:
data minimization;
prohibitions on advertising or promotional content
directed at a student
, including:
advertising products or services to a student while the student is using educational software;
allowing a third-party to advertise a product or a service to a student; and
the inclusion of advertising or promotional content within educational software accessible to a student;
limits on secondary data use;
security safeguards;
breach notifications;
data retention and deletion requirements; and
directory information protections;
complies with the sensitive materials requirements described in Section 53G-10-103;
requires that educational software may not display, recommend, algorithmically generate, or provide access to any instructional or supplemental content that constitutes:
pornographic or indecent material as that term is defined in Section 76-5c-208; or
sexual exploitation or abuse;
prohibits addictive design features;
prohibits a vendor from collecting, storing, or analyzing:
biometric identifiers, except for:
voice recognition for speech-to-text accessibility features; or
other biometric data explicitly required for a student's IEP or Section 504 accommodation plan; and
behavioral or emotional signals for purposes of:
psychological profiling;
emotional manipulation;
commercial marketing or advertising; or
any purpose other than improving educational outcomes;
provides that any data collected under Subsection (1)(f):
is disclosed in the statewide digital privacy agreement;
is the minimum amount necessary for the educational purpose;
is not used for commercial purposes; and
is subject to strict security safeguards;
requires a vendor to:
use encryption for data in transit and at rest;
disclose the use of sub-processors; and

disclose to the contracting entity all data elements collected, third-party recipients, embedded libraries and analytics tools, device-level permissions, and artificial intelligence components and functions;
prohibits educational software from accessing a device's camera and microphone unless:
necessary for an educational function; and
disclosed in the digital privacy agreement;
prohibits a vendor from conditioning access, features, pricing, or support on a:
usage quota; or
screen-time expectation;
includes a termination-for-cause provision that:
requires the vendor to cure any violation of the digital privacy agreement within a timeline the state board establishes;
authorizes the contracting entity to terminate the contract if the vendor fails to cure the violation of the digital privacy agreement required under Subsection (1)(k)(i);
provides that the termination described in this Subsection (1)(k) may occur without penalty, early-termination fee, or additional obligation to the contracting entity;
requires the vendor to acknowledge that termination under this Subsection (1)(k) does not constitute a breach by the contracting entity; and

when a vendor fails to cure as required under Subsection (1)(k)(i), authorizes the state board to direct the contracting entity to terminate the contract or terminate the contracting entity's participation in the contract on the contracting entity's behalf; and

prohibits educational software that a vendor designs for peer-to-peer communication unless the educational software includes an administrative control that enables an administrator to turn peer-to-peer communication on or off.

Notwithstanding the requirements of Subsection (1) the state board may allow a contracting entity to modify the statewide data privacy agreement if the state board determines the modification to be necessary for a contract with a specific vendor.
A contracting entity may use administrative software without executing the statewide digital privacy agreement.
Before a contracting entity uses administrative software, the contracting entity shall execute a digital privacy agreement that includes the requirements described in Subsection (1)(b).

This part does not apply to a telecommunications carrier or internet service provider, or to any affiliate of the telecommunication carrier or internet service provider, when acting solely as a passive conduit for the transmission, routing, or provision of internet connectivity or network access for software or digital services a student uses, including:
the transmission or routing of data packets;
the provision of wireless or broadband connectivity;
network management, quality-of-service, cybersecurity, or fraud-prevention functions; or
the provision of device-level operating systems or firmware updates that are not designed to collect, analyze, or monetize student data.

section.secline: Section 3. Section 53G-7-1403 is enacted to read:

subsection.amend: Before the vendor allows a contracting entity to install, assign, or otherwise make the educational software available for student use, the vendor shall:
execute the statewide digital privacy agreement; and

subject to Section 53G-7-1407, verify that the educational software is academically effective through self-verification or independent verification, in accordance with the process the state board creates in Section 53G-7-1405.
A vendor that self-certifies for academic effectiveness shall:
submit to the state board a written certification that the educational software is academically effective; and
comply with the rules and timelines the state board establishes under Section 53G-7-1405.
A vendor shall:
provide the state board access to all records, documents, and data necessary to complete the audits described in Section 53G-7-1405; and
execute the statewide digital privacy agreement before providing educational software or digital services to a contracting entity.
A vendor may appeal a finding of noncompliance, issued under Section 53G-7-1405, through the administrative process the state board establishes.
A vendor-proposed privacy agreement, end-user license agreement, click-through terms, terms of service, or substitute contract is void and unenforceable with respect to student data or student use.
A vendor may not:
alter, supplement, replace, or modify the statewide digital privacy agreement; or
request or require that a parent or contracting entity:
waive any right under this part;
agree to arbitration that limits this part; or
accept liability limitations inconsistent with this part.

section.secline: Section 4. Section 53G-7-1404 is enacted to read:

subsection.amend: A contracting entity shall:
execute the statewide digital privacy agreement for any educational software the contracting entity adopts;

unless the entity is contracting for an educational software that the state board has previously approved and listed on the list described in Subsection 53G-7-1405(1)(a)(iv), obtain documentation of a vendor's verification of academic effectiveness, in accordance with the timelines and rules the state board establishes under Section 53G-7-1405, before continuing use of the educational software for an individual to:
install;
assign; or
make available for student use;
submit to the state board for listing:
the executed statewide digital privacy agreement required under Subsection (1)(a); and
if necessary under Subsection (1)(b), the verification documentation described in Subsection (1)(b); and
provide the state board access to all records, documents, and data necessary to complete the audits described in Section 53G-7-1405.
A contracting entity may appeal a finding of noncompliance the state board issues under Section 53G-7-1405 through the administrative process the state board establishes.
A contracting entity that is not the state board may request a modification to the statewide data privacy agreement in accordance with Subsection 53G-7-1402(2).
Except as provided in Subsection (4)(a), a contracting entity may not alter, supplement, replace, or modify the statewide digital privacy agreement.

A contracting entity shall ensure that a digital privacy agreement between a vendor and a contracting entity executed before January 1, 2028, complies with the requirements of this section before July 1, 2029.

Between January 1, 2028, and July 1, 2029, a contracting entity may continue to use an existing digital privacy agreement if the contracting entity actively works toward compliance with the statewide digital privacy agreement.

section.secline: Section 5. Section 53G-7-1405 is enacted to read:

catline.bold: 53G-7-1405. State board and local education agencies -- Compliance -- Duties.

subsection.amend: The state board shall:
ensure that educational software is not available for use in student instruction without verifying the software for academic effectiveness;
maintain a public list of independent evaluators that a vendor may use to verify educational software as academically effective;
place educational software on the master list described in Subsection (1)(a)(iv) when a contracting entity, in accordance with Section 53G-7-1404:
executes a statewide digital privacy agreement; and
verifies that the educational software is academically effective;
create and maintain a master list of all educational software the state board approves for student use under this section; and
before July 1, 2028, create a process that allows a vendor to demonstrate academic effectiveness through:
vendor self-verification; or
third-party verification.
The state board may consult software vendors for technical input regarding functionality or implementation of the requirements of this section.
An educational software's exclusion from the master list does not prevent a contracting entity from using the software if the software meets the requirements of Section 53G-7-1404.
An educational software's inclusion on the master list described in Subsection (1)(a)(iii) does not constitute state board approval or endorsement.
The state board shall:
monitor and enforce compliance with this section through periodic audits of:
contracting entities; and
vendors;

beginning July 1, 2029, audit each LEA, at least once every three years, to confirm that, for every educational software product students use that is not on the state board approved list described in Subsection (1)(a)(v), the LEA has:
executed the statewide digital privacy agreement; and
obtained the verification documentation;
in performing the audits required under Subsection (2)(a)(i), review vendor compliance with:
the requirements of this part; and
Title 53E, Chapter 9, Student Privacy and Data Protection; and
issue a written compliance report, following each audit required under this Subsection (2)(a), identifying:
findings of compliance and noncompliance;
required corrective actions; and
applicable timelines for remediation.
The state board may publish audit findings under Subsection (2)(a) to:
promote transparency; and
make the public aware of compliant and noncompliant practices.
If the state board finds an LEA to be out of compliance with the requirements of Section 53G-7-1404, the LEA shall:
discontinue use of the noncompliant educational software;
remedy the source of the noncompliance; and
implement a corrective-action plan to prevent future violations.
The state board shall provide:
technical guidance and transition support to contracting entities and vendors regarding the transition to the statewide digital privacy agreement and academic effectiveness requirements; and
implementation timelines and instructions necessary for contracting entities to achieve compliance.
The state board may prioritize technical guidance and transition support for:
vendors executing digital privacy agreements with multiple contracting entities;
statewide or consortium contracts; or
software with known privacy, safety, or effectiveness concerns.
An LEA may not use software other than educational software in a public school for instruction of a student.

Before an LEA enters into a digital privacy agreement with a vendor for educational software, the LEA shall ensure that the digital privacy agreement meets each of the requirements of the statewide digital privacy agreement the state board creates under Section 53G-7-1402.
An LEA shall:
provide a parent, annually, with a list of all educational software products:
for which the vendor has executed a statewide digital privacy agreement;
for which a vendor has completed the verification of academic effectiveness required under Section 53G-7-1403; and
that the LEA may assign, require, recommend, or otherwise made available for student use during the upcoming school year;
ensure that the list described in Subsection (8)(a) includes, at minimum:
the product name and vendor;
the educational software's primary instructional purpose;
a link to the educational software's statewide digital privacy agreement; and
except as provided in Section 53G-7-1407, a link to the academic effectiveness verification a vendor is required to produce under Section 53G-7-1403;
publish the list described in Subsection (8)(a) on the LEA's public website;
update the list described in Subsection (8)(a) within 10 business days of any addition or removal of an educational software product;
for any educational software added during the course of the school year:
provide written notice to parents within 10 school days of the products approval;
include links to the product's statewide digital privacy agreement and academic-effectiveness verification summary; and
provide the notice, described in Subsection (8)(e)(i), before assigning the educational software or making it available for student use;
provide parents with written notice of any significant software update or change in data-collection or data-sharing practices that:
may affect compliance with the statewide digital privacy agreement; or
may trigger new consent requirements under state or federal law; and
maintain a publicly accessible archive of educational software that students previously used, including:
the software name and vendor; and
the dates during which the product was in active use.
In accordance with Title 63G, Chapter 3, Utah Administrative Rulemaking Act, the state board shall make rules to implement the requirements of this part, including rules to:
create a statewide digital privacy agreement;
create an administrative process for a parent to submit a complaint in accordance with Section 53G-7-1406;
create a process for vendors and contracting entities to appeal a finding of noncompliance;
before January 1, 2028, create a process for ensuring all educational software is academically effective, including:
classifying software based on whether the software is:
student-facing and if the student uses the software directly for instruction, practice, assessment, feedback, communication, collaboration, accessibility, or other learning-related interaction; or
used solely for administrative or technical infrastructure functions;

requiring evaluation of educational software within each classification and establishing classification-specific methodologies or evidentiary standards for determining whether educational software provides, enables, or supports:
active learning;
skill development; or
academic progress; and
creating the verification process described in Subsection (1)(a)(v);
create standards and a process for approving and listing the educational software described in Subsection (1)(a)(v);
create and maintain the master list described in Subsection (1)(a)(iii);
conduct the audits required under Subsection (2)(a); and
create a process for receiving and responding to complaints a parent submits under Section 53G-7-1406.

section.secline: Section 6. Section 53G-7-1406 is enacted to read:

subsection.amend: A parent may submit a written complaint to the state board alleging:
a contracting entity using educational software without executing a statewide digital privacy agreement;
a contracting entity using educational software without being verified as academically effective; or
a vendor's violation of the statewide digital privacy agreement.
Upon receiving a complaint described in Subsection (1)(a), the state board shall consult with the Office of the Attorney General to:
review the complaint;
determine if a violation has occurred;
notify the parent of the determination; and
take appropriate enforcement action under this part if noncompliance is found.
A court shall award the Office of the Attorney General reasonable attorney fees, court costs, and investigative expenses incurred in an action under this part.

section.secline: Section 7. Section 53G-7-1407 is enacted to read:

subsection.amend: Notwithstanding Subsection 53G-7-1403(1)(a)(ii) and before July 1, 2028, a vendor may make educational software available for use to a contracting entity on a provisional basis for up to 24 months from the initial deployment of the software if:
the vendor submits a verification plan to the contracting entity before the educational software is made available for student use, including:
a proposed methodology for demonstrating academic effectiveness;
a timeline for completion of verification of academic effectiveness; and
interim measures to assess active learning, skill development, or academic progress in the intended subject area; and
the vendor demonstrates to the contracting entity that the educational software:
aligns with state core education standards; and
meets an educational purpose.
A student may not use educational software made available under Subsection (1)(a) without consent from the student's parent.
A contracting entity shall notify a student's parent of:
the implementation of educational software on a provisional basis in accordance with Subsection (1)(a); and
the consent required to use the educational software under Subsection (1)(b).

During the provisional period described in Subsection (1)(a), the contracting entity and vendor shall collect data necessary for academic effectiveness verification in accordance with the statewide digital privacy agreement.

sectionText.amend: This bill takes effect on July 1, 2026.
